content: The function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\sec \left[\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)\right]$ is________function
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} f(-x) & =\sec \left[\log \left(-x+\sqrt{1+(-x)^2}\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[\log \left(-x+\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[\log \left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-x\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[\log \left(\frac{1+x^2-x^2}{\sqrt{1+x^2}+x}\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[\log \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}+x}\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[-\log \left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)\right] \\ & =\sec \left[\log \left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)\right] \\ & =\mathrm{f}(x) \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) \text { is an even function. } \end{aligned} $$

content: If $[x]^2-5[x]+6=0$, where [.] denotes the greatest integer function, then
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & {[x]^2-5[x]+6=0} \\ & \Rightarrow[x]^2-3[x]-2[x]+6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow([x]-3)([x]-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow[x]=3 \quad \text { or } \quad[x]=2 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[3,4) \quad \text { or } \quad x \in[2,3) \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[2,4) \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $\mathrm{f}: \mathbb{R}-\{2\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}-\{1\}$ defined by $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-2}$ and $\mathrm{g}: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $\mathrm{g}(x)=3 x-2$, then sum of all values of $x$ for which $\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)+\mathrm{g}^{-1}(x)=\frac{19}{6}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-2} \\ & \Rightarrow x y-2 y=x-3 \\ & \Rightarrow x y-x=2 y-3 \\ & \Rightarrow x(y-1)=2 y-3 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{2 y-3}{y-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{-1}(y)=\frac{2 y-3}{y-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)=\frac{2 x-3}{x-1} \\ & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{g}(x)=3 x-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{y+2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~g}^{-1}(y)=\frac{y+2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~g}^{-1}(x)=\frac{x+2}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)+\mathrm{g}^{-1}(x)=\frac{19}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 x-3}{x-1}+\frac{x+2}{3}=\frac{19}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^2-5 x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(2 x+1)(x-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{-1}{2} \text { or } x=3 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Sum of all values of } x=\frac{-1}{2}+3=\frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$ and $\mathrm{g}(x)=\frac{3 x+x^3}{1+3 x^2}$, then $(\mathrm{fog})(x)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(x)=f(g(x)) & =f\left(\frac{3 x+x^3}{1+3 x^2}\right) \\ & =\log \left(\frac{1+\frac{3 x+x^3}{1+3 x^2}}{1-\frac{3 x+x^3}{1+3 x^2}}\right) \\ & =\log \left(\frac{1+3 x^2+3 x+x^3}{1+3 x^2-3 x-x^3}\right) \\ & =\log \frac{(1+x)^3}{(1-x)^3} \\ & =\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^3 \\ & =3 \mathrm{f}(x) \end{aligned} $$

content:
$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 3-x, & -1 \leqslant x<0 \\ 1+\frac{5 x}{3}, & -3 \leqslant x \leqslant 2 \end{array}\right. \text { and } \\ & g(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} -x, & -2 \leqslant x \leqslant 3 \\ x, & 0 \leqslant x \leqslant 1 \end{aligned}\right. \end{aligned} $$

then range of (fog) $(x)$ is
explanation:
$$ g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -x, & -2 \leq x \leq 3 \\ x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \end{array}\right. $$

A function cannot be defined with two different outputs at the same input

$$ \therefore \quad g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} -x & \text { for }-2 < x \leq 0 \\ x & \text { for } 0 \leq x < 1 \\ -x & \text { for } 1 \leq x \leq 3 \end{array}\right. $$

Case I: $-2 < x \leq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & g(x)=-x \\ & f(g(x))=f(-x), x \in[-2,0] \\ \therefore \quad & f(-x)=1+\frac{5(-x)}{3}=1-\frac{5 x}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Range of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{n}))$ is $\left[1, \frac{13}{3}\right]$

Case II: $0 < x < 1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}(x)=x \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x))=\mathrm{f}(x), x \in(0,1) \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x))=1+\frac{5 x}{3} \end{aligned} $$

Range of $f(g(n))=\left[1, \frac{8}{3}\right]$

Case III: $1 \leq x < 3$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}(x)=-x \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x))=\mathrm{f}(-x) \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x))=1-\frac{5 \mathrm{n}}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Range of $f(g(x))=\left[-4, \frac{-2}{3}\right]$

$\therefore \quad$ Combined range of $f(g(n))=\left[-4, \frac{13}{3}\right]$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=3 x+10, \mathrm{~g}(x)=x^2-1$, then $(\mathrm{fog})^{-1}(x)=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} (\mathrm{fog})(x) & =\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{~g}(x)) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(x^2-1\right) \\ & =3\left(x^2-1\right)+10 \\ & =3 x^2+7 \end{aligned} \\ & \text { Let } y=3 x^2+7 \\ & \therefore \quad x^2=\frac{y-7}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{y-7}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{fog})^{-1}(y)=\left(\frac{y-7}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{fog})^{-1}(x)=\left(\frac{x-7}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{aligned} $$

content: The values of $b$ and $c$ for which the identity $\mathrm{f}(x+1)-\mathrm{f}(x)=8 x+3$ is satisfied, where $\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{b} x^2+\mathrm{c} x+\mathrm{d}$, are
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given that } \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{b} x^2+\mathrm{c} x+\mathrm{d}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x+1)-\mathrm{f}(x)=8 x+3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}(x+1)^2+\mathrm{c}(x+1)+\mathrm{d}-\left(\mathrm{b} x^2+\mathrm{c} x+\mathrm{d}\right)=8 x+1 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~b} x+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=8 x+3 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~b}=8 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=4 \\ & \text { Also, } \mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=-1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content:
For a real number $x,[x]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $x$. Then the value of

$$ \begin{array}{r} {\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]+\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{100}\right]+\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{100}\right]+\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{100}\right]+} \left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{99}{100}\right]= \end{array} $$
explanation:
Given expression

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sum_{i=0}^{99}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i}{100}\right] \\ & =\sum_{i=0}^{49}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i}{100}\right]+\sum_{i=50}^{99}\left[\frac{2}{3}+\frac{i}{100}\right] \\ & =0+\sum_{i=50}^{99}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i}{100}\right] \quad \ldots\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \frac{1}{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}+\frac{i}{100}<1 \\ \text { For } i=0,1,2, \ldots 49 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned} $$

$=50$ [each term in the summation is one or more than 1 but less than 2 when $\mathrm{i}=50,51,52$, ….99]

content: The function defined by $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{2 x+3}{3 x+4}, x \neq-\frac{4}{3}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Assume } \mathrm{f}\left(x_1\right)=\mathrm{f}\left(x_2\right)\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{2 x_1+3}{3 x_1+4}=\frac{2 x_2+3}{3 x_2+4} \\ \therefore & \left(2 x_1+3\right)\left(3 x_2+4\right)= \\ \therefore & 8 x_1+9 x_2=8 x_2+9 x_1 \\ \therefore & x_1=x_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Function is one-or } \\ & \text { Let } y=\mathrm{f}(x) \\ & y=\frac{2 x+3}{3 x+4} \\ \therefore & y(3 x+4)=2 x+3 \\ \therefore & 3 x y+4 y=2 x+3 \\ \therefore & 3 x y-2 x=3-4 y \\ \therefore & x=\frac{3-4 y}{3 y-2} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ For every $y$ in the co-domain, there exists an $x$ in the domain such that

$\mathrm{f}(x)=y$ except at $y=\frac{2}{3}$

$\Rightarrow$ Function is onto for $y \neq \frac{2}{3}$

content: Range of the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x^2+x+2}{x^2+x+1}, x \in \mathbb{R}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\frac{x^2+x+2}{x^2+x+1} \\ & \Rightarrow(y-1) x^2+(y-1) x+y-2=0 \end{aligned}$$

For real value of $x, \mathrm{~b}^2-4 \mathrm{ac} \geq 0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(y-1)^2-4(y-1)(y-2) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y^2-10 y+7 \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow(y-1)(3 y-7) \leq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1 \leq y \leq \frac{7}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Here, $y \neq 1$ for any $x \in \mathrm{R}$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}=\left(1, \frac{7}{3}\right]$$

content: If $[x]^2-5[x]+6=0$, where $[\cdot]$ denotes the greatest integer function, then
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given: }\\ &\begin{aligned} & {[x]^2-5[x]+6=0} \\ & \text { Let }[x]=\mathrm{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2-5 \mathrm{a}+6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}-2)(\mathrm{a}-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2 \text { or } \mathrm{a}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow[x]=2 \text { or }[x]=3 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[2,3) \text { or } x \in[3,4) \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[2,4) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=(x+1)^2-1, x \geqslant-1$, then the set $\left\{x / f(x)=f^{-1}(x)\right\}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=x \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{f}(x)+1)^2-1=x \\ & \Rightarrow\left[(x+1)^2-1+1\right]^2-1=x \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)^4-1=x \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)^4-(x+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)\left[(x+1)^3-1\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x+1=0 \text { or }(x+1)^3=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-1 \text { or } x=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}:[1, \infty) \rightarrow[2, \infty)$ is given by $\mathrm{f}(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}$ then $\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)$ equals
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=x+\frac{1}{x} \\ & \text { let } y=x+\frac{1}{x} \\ \therefore \quad & x y=x^2+1 \\ \therefore \quad & x^2-x y+1=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & x=\frac{y \pm \sqrt{y^2-4}}{2} \\ \therefore & x=\frac{y+\sqrt{y^2-4}}{2} \quad \ldots[\because x \in[1, \infty)]\\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)=\frac{x+\sqrt{x^2-4}}{2} \end{array}$$

content: If $[x]^2-5[x]+6=0$, where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer function, then
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given: }\\ &\begin{aligned} & {[x]^2-5[x]+6=0} \\ & \text { Let }[x]=\mathrm{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2-5 \mathrm{a}+6=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}-2)(\mathrm{a}-3)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=2 \text { or } \mathrm{a}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow[x]=2 \text { or }[x]=3 \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[2,3) \text { or } x \in[3,4) \\ & \Rightarrow x \in[2,4) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{a x}{x+1}, x \neq-1$, then for $\alpha=$ ________, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=x$.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\alpha x}{x+1} ; \\ & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}\right)=\frac{\alpha\left(\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}\right)}{\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}+1} \\ & \text { But } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=x_{\quad} \\ \therefore\quad& \frac{\alpha^2 x}{\alpha x+x+1}=x \\ & \operatorname{In} \text { L.H.S., Put } \alpha=-1, \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{(-1)^2 x}{(-1) x+x+1}=\frac{x}{-x+x+1}=x ; \\ \therefore \quad & \alpha=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The domain of definition of the function $y(x)$ is given by the equation $2^x+2^y=2$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & 2^x+2^y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^y=2-2^x \\ & \Rightarrow y=\log _2\left(2-2^x\right) \text { is defined, if } 2-2^x>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^x<2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{x-1}<1 \\ & \Rightarrow x-1<0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\infty< x<1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The domain of definition of the function $f(x)$ given by the equation $2^x+2^y=2$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & 2^x+2^y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^y=2-2^x \\ & \Rightarrow y=\log _2\left(2-2^x\right) \text { is defined, if } 2-2^x >0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^x<2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{x-1}<1 \\ & \Rightarrow x-1<0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\infty< x<1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{2-x}, \mathrm{~g}(x)=\frac{x+1}{x+2}$, then (gogof) $(x)=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x}{2-x}, \mathrm{~g}(x)=\frac{x+1}{x+2}\\ &(g \circ f)(x)=\frac{\frac{x}{2-x}+1}{\frac{x}{2-x}+2}\\ &\text { (gogof) }(x)=\frac{\frac{\frac{x}{2-x}+1}{\frac{x}{2-x}+2}+1}{\frac{\frac{x}{2-x}+1}{\frac{x}{2-x}+2}+2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\frac{x+2-x}{x+4-2 x}+1}{\frac{x+2-x}{x+4-2 x}+2} \\ & =\frac{\frac{2}{4-x}+1}{\frac{2}{4-x}+2} \\ & =\frac{2+4-x}{2+8-2 x} \\ & =\frac{6-x}{10-2 x} \end{aligned}$$

content: The domain of definition of $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\log _2(x+3)}{x^2+3 x+2}$ is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\log _2(x+3)}{x^2+3 x+2}$

Now, $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is defined if $x^2+3 x+2 \neq 0$

$\Rightarrow(x+1)(x+2) \neq 0$

$\Rightarrow x \neq-1,-2$.

Also, $x+3>0$

$x>-3$

$\therefore \quad x \in(-3, \infty)-\{-1,-2\}$

content: If $g(x)=x^2+x-1$ and (gof) $(x)=4 x^2-10 x+5$, then $\mathrm{f}(2)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}(x)=x^2+x-1 \\ & \mathrm{~g}[\mathrm{f}(x)]=4 x^2-10 x+5 \\ \therefore\quad& {[\mathrm{f}(x)]^2+\mathrm{f}(x)-1=4 x^2-10 x+5} \end{aligned}$$

for $x=2$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & {[\mathrm{f}(2)]^2+\mathrm{f}(2)-1=1} \\ \therefore\quad& {[\mathrm{f}(2)]^2+\mathrm{f}(2)-2=0} \\ \therefore\quad& {[\mathrm{f}(2)+2][\mathrm{f}(2)-1]=0} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore\quad f(2)=-2$ or 1

content: For a suitable chosen real constant a, let a function $f: \mathbb{R}-\{-\mathrm{a}\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be defined by $f(x)=\frac{a-x}{a+x}$. Further suppose that for any real number $x \neq-\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{f}(x) \neq-\mathrm{a}$, (fof) $(x)=x$. Then $f\left(-\frac{1}{5}\right)$ is equal to
explanation:
Given: $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{a}-x}{\mathrm{a}+x}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{f}(x)}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{f}(x)}=x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}-x}{\mathrm{a}+x}\right)}{\mathrm{a}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}-x}{\mathrm{a}+x}\right)}=x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{a}+x}{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{a}-x}=x \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{a}^2-\mathrm{a}\right)+(\mathrm{a}+1) x=\left(\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{a}\right) x+(\mathrm{a}-1) x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}-1) x^2+\left(\mathrm{a}^2-1\right) x-\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{a}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}-1)\left[x^2+(\mathrm{a}+1) x-\mathrm{a}\right]=0 \end{aligned}$$

This is possible when $\mathrm{a}=1$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad f(x)= \frac{1-x}{1+x} \\ & \begin{aligned} f\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right) & =\frac{1-\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)}{1+\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)} \\ & =\frac{1+\frac{1}{5}}{1-\frac{1}{5}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{6}{5}}{\frac{4}{5}}=\frac{6}{4}=1.5 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{2 x-3}{3 x-4}, x \neq \frac{4}{3}$$, then the value of $$\mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=y \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(y)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{2 x-3}{3 x-4} \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3 x y-4 y=2 x-3 \\\\ & \Rightarrow x(3 y-2)=4 y-3 \\\\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{4 y-3}{3 y-2} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{-1}(y)=\frac{4 y-3}{3 y-2} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{-1}(x)=\frac{4 x-3}{3 x-2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The range of the function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x^2}{x^2+1}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } y=\frac{x^2}{x^2+1} \\ & \Rightarrow y x^2+y=x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2(y-1)+y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{y}{1-y} \end{aligned}$$

For $$x$$ to be real,

$$\begin{aligned} & y(1-y) \geq 0 \text { and } 1-y \neq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow y(y-1) \leq 0 \text { and } y \neq 1 \\ & \Rightarrow 0 \leq y<1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=[x] \cdot \cos \left(\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right) \pi$$, where $$[\cdot]$$ denotes the greatest integer function, is discontinuous at

options.content: every rational $$x$$ which is not an integer.

explanation:
Greatest integer function is discontinuous on integer values.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x)$$ is discontinuous at all integer points.

content: The domain of the definition of the function $$y(x)$$ is given by the equation $$2^x+2^y=2$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 2^x+2^y=2 \\ & 2^y=2-2^x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{l} \because & \quad 2^y>0 \quad \text{[exponential function]}\\ \therefore & \quad 2-2^x>0 \\ & \quad 2^x<2 \\ & \quad x<1 \\ \therefore & x \in(-\infty, 1) \end{array}$$

content: If $$3 \mathrm{f}(x)-\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=8 \log _2 x^3, x>0$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(2), \mathrm{f}(4)$$, $$f(8)$$ are in
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{f}(x)-\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=8 \log _2 x^3 \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)-\mathrm{f}(x)=8 \log _2\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^3 \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 8 \mathrm{f}(x)=24 \log _2 x^3+8 \log _2\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{f}(x)=72 \log _2 x-24 \log _2 x \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{f}(x)=48 \log _2 x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)=6 \log _2 x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \quad f(2)=6 \log _2 2=6 \\ & f(4)=6 \log _2 4=12 \\ & f(8)=6 \log _2 8=18 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad f(2), f(4), f(8)$$ are in A.P.

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=x^2+1$$ and $$\mathrm{g}(x)=\frac{1}{x}$$, then the value of $$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(x))))$$ at $$x=1$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{f}(x)=x^2+1, & \mathrm{~g}(x)=\frac{1}{x} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(x)))) & =\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(x^2+1\right)\right)\right) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{1}{x^2+1}\right)\right) \\ & =\mathrm{f}\left(x^2+1\right) \\ & =\left(x^2+1\right)^2+1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ At $$x=1$$, we get the value of above function

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[(1)^2+1\right]^2+1 \\ & =5 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{g}(x)=1+\sqrt{x}$$ and $$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=3+2 \sqrt{x}+x$$ then $$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{g}(x)=1+\sqrt{x} \text { and } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=3+2 \sqrt{x}+x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=\left[(\sqrt{x})^2+2 \sqrt{x}+1\right]+2 \\ = & (\sqrt{x}+1)^2+2 \\ = & {[\mathrm{g}(x)]^2+2 } \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{f}(x)=x^2+2 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{f}(x))=\left(x^2+2\right)^2+2=x^4+4 x^2+6 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{3 x+4}{5 x-7}$$ and $$\mathrm{g}(x)=\frac{7 x+4}{5 x-3}$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} f(g(x)) & =f\left(\frac{7 x+4}{5 x-3}\right) \\ & =\frac{3\left(\frac{7 x+4}{5 x-3}\right)+4}{5\left(\frac{7 x+4}{5 x-3}\right)-7} \\ & =\frac{21 x+12+20 x-12}{35 x+20-35 x+21} \\ & =\frac{41 x}{41} \\ & =x \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(x))=\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{3 x+4}{5 x-7}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{7\left(\frac{3 x+4}{5 x-7}\right)+4}{5\left(\frac{3 x+4}{5 x-7}\right)-3} \\ & =\frac{21 x+28+20 x-28}{15 x+20-15 x+21} \\ & =\frac{41 x}{41} \\ & =x \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore\quad\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x))=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(x))$$

content: The domain of the function given by $$2^x+2^y=2$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & 2^x+2^y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^y=2-2^x \\ & \Rightarrow y=\log _2\left(2-2^x\right) \text { is defined, if } 2-2^x > 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^x < 2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{x-1} < 1 \\ & \Rightarrow x-1 < 0 \\ & \Rightarrow-\infty < x < 1 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R} ; \mathrm{g}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$$ are two functions such that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x-3, \mathrm{~g}(x)=x^3+5$$, then $$(\mathrm{fog})^{-1}(-9)$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\mathrm{f}(x)=2 x-3, \mathrm{~g}(x)=x^3+5$$

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{fog}(x) & =\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(x)) \\ & =2 \mathrm{~g}(x)-3 \\ & =2\left(x^3+5\right)-3=2 x^3+7 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$(\mathrm{fog})(x)=y=2 x^3+7$$

$$\begin{aligned} & y=2 x^3+7 \\ & \Rightarrow y-7=2 x^3 \\ & \Rightarrow x^3=\frac{y-7}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{y-7}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \\ \therefore \quad & (\text { fog })^{-1}(y)=\left(\frac{y-7}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \\ \therefore \quad & (\text { fog })^{-1}(-9)=\left(\frac{-9-7}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=(-8)^{\frac{1}{3}}=-2 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\mathrm{f}: \mathbb{R}-\left(-\frac{3}{5}\right) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ is defined by $$f(x)=\frac{3 x-2}{5 x+3}$$, then $$f \circ f(1)$$ is
If $$[x]$$ is greatest integer function and $$2[2 x-5]-1=7$$, then $$x$$ lies in
explanation:
Let's start by solving the given equation step by step. We have: $$2[2x - 5] - 1 = 7$$

First, we add 1 to both sides: $$2[2x - 5] = 8$$

Then, we divide both sides by 2: $$[2x - 5] = 4$$

The greatest integer function $$[y]$$ outputs the greatest integer less than or equal to $$y$$. If $$[y] = n$$, where $$n$$ is an integer, this implies that $$n \leq y < n+1$$. Therefore, $$[2x - 5] = 4$$ implies that: $$4 \leq 2x - 5 < 5$$

Now let's solve for $$x$$ by adding 5 to all parts of the inequality: $$4 + 5 \leq 2x < 5 + 5$$ $$9 \leq 2x < 10$$

Finally, we divide all parts of the inequality by 2 to solve for $$x$$: $$\frac{9}{2} \leq x < \frac{10}{2}$$ $$\frac{9}{2} \leq x < 5$$

This means that $$x$$ can be equal to or greater than $$\frac{9}{2}$$, but must be less than 5. Hence, the correct option which portrays this interval is:

Option D: $$\left[\frac{9}{2}, 5\right)$$ where $$x$$ is greater than or equal to $$\frac{9}{2}$$ and less than 5.

content: If $$f(x)=2\{x\}+5 x$$, where $$\{x\}$$ is fractional part function, then $$f(-1.4)$$ is
explanation:
We know that $$\mathrm{x}=[\mathrm{x}]+\{\mathrm{x}\}$$

When $$x=-1.4$$, we get $$[x]=-2$$ and $$\{x\}=0.6$$

We have $$f(x)=2\{x\}+5 x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have } f(x)=2\{x . \\ & \therefore f(-1.4)=2(0.6)+5(-1.4)=-7+1.2=-5.8 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\frac{x}{2 x+1}$$ and $$g(x)=\frac{x}{x+1}$$, then $$(f \circ g)(x)=$$
explanation:
To find $$(f \circ g)(x)$$, we need to compose the functions $$f(x)$$ and $$g(x)$$. This means we will first apply $$g(x)$$, and then apply $$f$$ to the result of $$g(x)$$.

Given:

$$f(x)=\frac{x}{2x+1}$$

and

$$g(x)=\frac{x}{x+1}$$

First, compute $$g(x)$$ for a general $$x$$:

$$g(x) = \frac{x}{x+1}$$

Now, use this result as the input for $$f$$. Therefore, $$f(g(x))$$ is given by substituting $$g(x)$$ into $$f(x)$$:

$$f(g(x)) = f\left(\frac{x}{x+1}\right)$$

According to the definition of $$f(x)$$:

$$f(t) = \frac{t}{2t+1}$$

Here, $$t = \frac{x}{x+1}$$. Substitute $$t$$ into the equation for $$f(t)$$:

$$f\left(\frac{x}{x+1}\right) = \frac{\frac{x}{x+1}}{2 \left(\frac{x}{x+1}\right) + 1}$$

Simplify the inside of the fraction:

$$2 \left(\frac{x}{x+1}\right) + 1 = \frac{2x}{x+1} + 1 = \frac{2x + (x+1)}{x+1} = \frac{3x+1}{x+1}$$

Therefore,

$$f(g(x)) = \frac{\frac{x}{x+1}}{\frac{3x+1}{x+1}}$$

When you divide fractions, you multiply by the reciprocal:

$$f(g(x)) = \frac{x}{x+1} \cdot \frac{x+1}{3x+1} = \frac{x}{3x+1}$$

Hence,

$$(f \circ g)(x) = \frac{x}{3x+1}$$

The correct answer is Option B:

$$\frac{x}{3x+1}$$

content: The domain of the function $$\log _{10}\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\log _{10}\left(x^2-5 x+6\right) \\ & \therefore x^2-5 x+6>0 \Rightarrow(x-2)(x-3)>0 \Rightarrow x>3 \text { or } x<2 \\ & \therefore x \in(-\infty, 2) \cup(3, \infty) \end{aligned}$$

content: Range of the function $$f(x)=3+2^x+4^x$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=3+2^x+4^x=y \quad \text{... (say)}\\ & \text { Let } 2^x=a \Rightarrow 4^x=a^2 \\ & \therefore a^2+a+(3-y)=0 \end{aligned}$$

As $$a \in R$$, we write

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { As }(1)^2-4(1)(3-y) \geq 0 \\ & \therefore 1-12+4 y \geq 0 \Rightarrow 4 y \geq 11 \Rightarrow y \geq \frac{11}{4} \\ & \text { Also } 2^x+4^x>0 \Rightarrow y \neq 3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Range from given options is $$(3, \infty)$$

content: If $$f(x)=[8 x]-3$$, where $$[x]$$ is greatest integer function of $$x$$, then $$f(\pi)=$$ (where $$\pi=3,14$$)
explanation:
The greatest integer function, denoted by $$[x]$$, returns the greatest integer less than or equal to $$x$$. To find $$f(\pi)$$ where $$\pi$$ is approximately equal to 3.14 (though more accurately $$\pi \approx 3.14159$$), we substitute $$\pi$$ into the given function $$f(x)=[8x]-3$$.

Given that the approximation for $$\pi$$ is 3.14, when calculating $$f(\pi)$$, we first compute the product inside the greatest integer function:

$$8\pi = 8 \times 3.14 = 25.12$$

The greatest integer less than or equal to 25.12 is 25 because the greatest integer function drops all decimal places and takes the largest integer not greater than the given number. So, $$[8\pi] = [25.12] = 25$$.

Substituting this result back into our function:

$$f(\pi) = [8\pi] - 3 = 25 - 3 = 22$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D, 22.

content: The domain of the function $$f(x)=\sqrt{x-1}+\sqrt{6-x}$$ is
explanation:
To determine the domain of the function $$f(x) = \sqrt{x-1} + \sqrt{6-x}$$, we need to find the set of all possible values of $$x$$ for which the function is defined.

For the square root function to be defined, the radicand (the expression inside the square root) must be non-negative. Therefore, we have two conditions to satisfy:

1. $$x - 1 \geq 0$$

  1. $$6 - x \geq 0$$

Let's solve each inequality separately:

1. Solving $$x - 1 \geq 0$$:

$$x \geq 1$$

2. Solving $$6 - x \geq 0$$:

$$x \leq 6$$

Combining these two conditions, we get:

$$1 \leq x \leq 6$$

Thus, the domain of the function $$f(x) = \sqrt{x-1} + \sqrt{6-x}$$ is $$[1, 6]$$.

The correct option is:

Option B

$$[1,6]$$

content: If f(x) = 3[x] + 5{x + 1}, where [x] is greatest integer function of x and {x} is fractional part function of x, then f($$-$$1.32) =
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \\ & f(x)=3[x]+5\{x+1\} \\ & x=-1.32 \Rightarrow[x]=[-1.32]=-2 \\ & \text{Also} \quad x+1=-1.32+1=-0.32 \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore & {[x+1]=[-0.32]=-1 \text { and }\{x+1\}=0.68 } \\ \therefore & f(x)=3(-2) +5(0.68) \\ & =-6+3.4=-2.6\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$$

content:
Let $$A=[a, b, c, d], B=[1,2,3]$$. Relation $$R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$$ are as follows :

$$\begin{aligned} & R_1=[(\mathrm{a}, 1),(\mathrm{b}, 2),(\mathrm{c}, 1),(\mathrm{d}, 2)] \\ & \mathrm{R}_2=[(\mathrm{a}, 1),(\mathrm{b}, 1),(\mathrm{c}, 1),(\mathrm{d}, 1)] \\ & \mathrm{R}_3=[(\mathrm{a}, 2),(\mathrm{b}, 3),(\mathrm{c}, 2),(\mathrm{d}, 2)] \\ & \mathrm{R}_4=[(\mathrm{a}, 1),(\mathrm{b}, 2),(\mathrm{a}, 2),(\mathrm{d}, 3)] \text {, then } \end{aligned}$$
explanation: We find that in R$$_4$$, f(a) = 1 and f(a) = 2. Hence R$$_4$$ is not a function.

content: The domain of the function $$f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+|x|}}$$ is
explanation:
$$f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+|x|}}$$

Here $$x+|x| \geq 0$$ and $$\sqrt{x+|x|} \neq 0$$

$$\therefore x+|x|>0$$

Now when $$x>0, x+|x|=x+x \Rightarrow 2 x>0$$.

When $$\mathrm{x}<0, \mathrm{x}+|\mathrm{x}|=\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}=0$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{x}>0$$ is the required domain.

content: The range of the function $f(x)=\frac{x-3}{5-x}, x \neq 5$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, } f(x)=\frac{x-3}{5-x}\\ &\begin{array}{rlrl} & \text { Put, } \quad f(x) =y \\ & \therefore \quad y =\frac{x-3}{5-x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 y-x y =x-3 \\ & x(y+1) =5 y+3 \\ & x =\frac{5 y+3}{y+1} \\ & \therefore \quad y \in \mathbf{R}-\{-1\} \end{array}\\ &\therefore \text { Range of } f(x)=R-\{-1\} \end{aligned}$$

content: For $$f(x)=[x]$$, where $$[x]$$ is the greatest integer function, which of the following is true, for every $$x \in \mathbf{R}$$
explanation:
We have,

$$f(x)=[x]$$

We know that, $$x=[x]+\{x\}$$

Where $$[x]$$ is greatest integer function and $$\{x\}$$ is fractional part

$$\begin{array}{cc} & =\{x\}=x-[x] \\ & 0 \leq\{x\}<1 \\ \therefore & 0 \leq x-[x]<1 \\ \Rightarrow & {[x] \leq x<[x]+1} \\ \Rightarrow & {[x]+1>x} \end{array}$$

content: The domain of the function $$f(x)=\sqrt{x}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & f(x) \text { is defined when } x \geq 0 \\ & \therefore \quad x \in[0, \infty) \\ & \therefore \text { Division of } f(x)=R^{+} \cup\{0\} \end{aligned}$$

content: The approximate value of the function $$f(x)=x^3-3 x+5$$ at $$x=1.99$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{gathered} f(x)=x^3-3 x+5 \\ f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-3 \end{gathered}$$

We know that,

$$f(x+\Delta x)=f(x)+\Delta x f^{\prime}(x)$$

Here, $$\quad x+\Delta x=1.99$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad x & =2 \Delta x=-0.01 \\ \therefore \quad f(1.99) & =f(2)-0.01 f^{\prime}(2) \\ f(1.99) & =(2)^3-3(2)+5-(0.01)(3(2)^2-3)) \\ & =(8-6+5)-(0.01)(9) \\ & =7-0.09=6.91 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\frac{2 x+3}{3 x-2}, x \neq \frac{2}{3}$$, then the function $$f$$ of is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\frac{2 x+3}{3 x-2} \\ f o f & =f(f(x))=\frac{2 f(x)+3}{3 f(x)-2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \text { fof } & =\frac{2\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3 x-2}\right)+3}{3\left(\frac{2 x+3}{3 x-2}\right)-2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad f \circ f & =\frac{4 x+6+9 x-6}{6 x+9-6 x+4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad f \circ f=\frac{13 x}{13} & =x=\text { an identity function. } \end{aligned}$$

content: The domain of the real valued function $f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{x-2}{3-x}}$ is......
explanation:
We have,

$$f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{x-2}{3-x}}$$

Clearly, $f(x)$ will be defined when

$$\begin{aligned} & 3-x>0 \Rightarrow x<3 \\ & \text { and } \quad x-2 \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \geq 2 \\ & \therefore \text{Domain of } f(x) \text{is }[2,3) \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

content: Which of the following function has period 2?
explanation:
To determine which function has a period of 2, we need to understand the general form of a cosine function and how its period is calculated. The cosine function is periodic, and its standard period is $2\pi$. If a cosine function is given in the form $\cos(bx)$, the period $T$ is calculated using the formula:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi}{|b|} $$

Now let's analyze each option:

Option A:

$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}x\right)$

The period is:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi}{\left|\frac{\pi}{3}\right|} = \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{3}} = 6 $$

Option B:

$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right)$

The period is:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi}{\left|\frac{\pi}{2}\right|} = \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{2}} = 4 $$

Option C:

$\cos(2\pi x)$

The period is:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi}{|2\pi|} = 1 $$

Option D:

$\cos(\pi x)$

The period is:

$$ T = \frac{2\pi}{|\pi|} = 2 $$

Thus, the function $\cos(\pi x)$ from Option D is the one with a period of 2.

content: If $f(x)=3 x+6, g(x)=4 x+k$ and $f \circ g(x)=g \circ f(x)$ then $k=$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=3 x+6, g(x)=4 x+k \\ & \text { Since, fog }(x)=\operatorname{gof}(x) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f(g)(x)=g(f(x)) \\ & \Rightarrow f(4 x+k)=g(3 x+6) \\ & \Rightarrow 3(4 x+k)+6=4(3 x+6)+k \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 k-k=24-6 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 k=18 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=9 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $f(x)=3 x-2$ and $g(x)=x^2$, then $(f \circ g)(x)=$
explanation:
We have, $f(x)=3 x-2, g(x)=x^2$

Then

$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(x) & =f(g(x))=f\left(x^2\right) \\ & =3 x^2-2 \end{aligned}$$
